title: FCC took pricey gifts from Paramount as the company needed approval for deals
gist: A ProPublica investigation finds FCC Chair Brendan Carr and Commissioner Olivia Trusty accepted lavish Kennedy Center gala tickets from Paramount (a $125,000-a-seat skybox for Carr; over $12,000 in tickets for Trusty) both before and after casting the deciding votes approving Paramount's $8 billion Skydance merger, and ethics experts warn the same conflict now hangs over the FCC's pending review of Paramount's $110 billion hostile bid for Warner Bros. Discovery.

insights.claim: At the December 2025 Kennedy Center Honors gala, FCC Chair Brendan Carr and his wife sat with Paramount CEO David Ellison in a private skybox where seats sold for $125,000 each, while Commissioner Olivia Trusty and a guest attended on tickets from Paramount worth more than $12,000, according to ethics disclosure records obtained by ProPublica.
insights.speaker: ProPublica
insights.so_what: Direct financial ties between the deciding regulators and the regulated party create a documentable conflict-of-interest record that opposing litigants (states, unions, rival bidders) can use to challenge any future FCC approval in court.
insights.locator: Five months earlier, Federal Communications Commissioner Olivia Trusty

insights.claim: Carr has accepted Kennedy Center gala tickets from CBS or Paramount at least seven times since his 2017 FCC appointment, totaling over $63,000 in gifts; seven of the ten FCC commissioners who served since 2016 accepted more than $260,000 combined in such tickets, with total gifts across broadcasters (including NBCUniversal, ABC-Disney, and Fox News for other events) topping $308,000.
insights.speaker: ProPublica
insights.so_what: This is a decade-long, bipartisan, institutional pattern rather than a one-off lapse, meaning the practice is unlikely to stop without a formal ethics-rule change or successful legal challenge, and continues to expose every pending FCC media merger to conflict-of-interest claims.
insights.locator: Despite their oversight role, FCC members

themes.theme: Merger approval traced to political leverage

insights.claim: After Paramount filed for FCC approval of its Skydance merger in September 2024 and Trump was announced as appointing Carr as incoming FCC chair in November 2024, Carr immediately called CBS 'biased' and an obstacle to the merger; CBS then settled Trump's lawsuit over a '60 Minutes' interview edit for $16 million, and two days after that payment was confirmed, the FCC took up the Paramount-Skydance merger, ultimately extracting a pledge from Paramount to eliminate its DEI initiatives and appoint an independent ombudsperson before approving the deal.
insights.speaker: ProPublica
insights.so_what: The timeline suggests merger approval was effectively linked to a political settlement rather than a neutral regulatory review, a pattern that could be replicated (or successfully attacked in court) in the pending Warner Bros. Discovery review.
insights.locator: More than a year earlier, in September 2024

insights.claim: The December 2025 Kennedy Center gala where Carr sat with Ellison came two days after Warner Bros. Discovery had initially accepted a rival bid from Netflix; that night Trump told reporters the Netflix deal 'could be a problem' and that he planned to get personally involved, and hours after the gala ended Paramount launched its hostile takeover bid for Warner Bros. Discovery. About three months later, Carr publicly endorsed Paramount over Netflix on CNBC, promising swift approval.
insights.speaker: ProPublica
insights.so_what: A sitting FCC chair publicly endorsing one bidder over another for a deal not yet formally before the commission is an unusual and legally risky signal that could be cited as evidence of predetermined bias if the WBD deal reaches litigation.
insights.locator: Warner at first rebuffed Paramount's overtures

insights.claim: Trusty and Carr both voted to approve the $8 billion Paramount-Skydance merger; Commissioner Anna Gomez voted against, calling the approval's conditions 'never-before-seen forms of government control over newsroom decisions and editorial judgment.'
insights.speaker: ProPublica
insights.so_what: The dissent on the record itself becomes ammunition for legal challengers arguing the approval process was compromised by both political pressure and personal gifts.
insights.quote: never-before-seen forms of government control over newsroom decisions and editorial judgment
insights.locator: Trusty and Carr voted in favor of the merger

themes.theme: Legal and governance risk to the pending Warner Bros. Discovery deal

insights.claim: Ethics experts (Walter Shaub, former head of the Office of Government Ethics; Virginia Canter, a former White House/Treasury/SEC ethics lawyer; and Kedric Payne of the Campaign Legal Center) say Carr and Trusty compromised the FCC's impartiality by accepting Paramount-funded gala tickets while Paramount matters were pending, and that both should recuse from any vote on the $110 billion Paramount-Warner Bros. Discovery deal.
insights.speaker: ProPublica
insights.so_what: If courts or the Justice Department credit this argument, the WBD deal's FCC approval path becomes materially riskier, adding tail risk to the ~$110B transaction's timeline and certainty.
insights.quote: There's no way that any top federal regulator should ever, ever accept a gift from a regulated company with interests their work will foreseeably affect. The appearance of taking gifts like that is terrible. What's at stake is nothing less than the public's trust in government.

insights.claim: Because the FCC currently has only three of five commissioner seats filled (Carr, Trusty, Gomez) and needs a three-member quorum to vote, any recusal by Carr or Trusty over the gift conflict could leave the commission unable to formally approve the Warner Bros. Discovery deal - though Carr could bypass a full commission vote by delegating approval authority to FCC staff, as he did with the Nexstar-Tegna acquisition.
insights.speaker: ProPublica
insights.so_what: The quorum mechanics create a structural workaround (staff-level delegation) that Paramount/Skydance could use to route around commissioner recusal, a scenario investors should model as the more likely approval path if legal pressure mounts.
insights.locator: If one or more commissioners choose to abstain

insights.claim: Former White House ethics attorney Richard Painter warned that a court could later find the FCC's merger decision invalid if the approval process is shown to have been corrupted by the gift-taking, stating 'a judge may very well say that the merger decision of the FCC isn't worth jack because the process was corrupted.'
insights.speaker: ProPublica
insights.so_what: Even a formally granted FCC approval may not be legally durable, meaning deal certainty for Paramount-WBD remains at risk well past any commission vote, into subsequent litigation.
insights.quote: A judge may very well say that the merger decision of the FCC isn't worth jack because the process was corrupted.
insights.locator: Richard Painter, a former White House ethics attorney

insights.claim: Federal ethics rules technically only require repayment of a gift's face value to cure a violation, not recusal from related votes - meaning Carr and Trusty could satisfy the letter of the gift rules cheaply while still participating in any Warner Bros. Discovery vote, according to former OGE head Walter Shaub.
insights.speaker: ProPublica
insights.so_what: The technical loophole for curing gift violations suggests recusal is unlikely absent external legal or political pressure, meaning the same commissioners who approved Skydance are likely to also decide the WBD deal.
insights.quote: If you repay the face value of the ticket, the gift rules don’t require you to recuse — though common sense and any kind of conscience might lead you to recuse voluntarily for the good of the country.
insights.locator: Federal rules only require those who accept improper gifts

themes.theme: Scale and opposition to the Paramount-WBD combination

insights.claim: The pending Paramount-Warner Bros. Discovery deal is valued at $110 billion and would combine Paramount+ and HBO Max streaming, CBS and CNN, and multiple film studios/cable/digital platforms under one company, with the FCC's review separately examining Middle Eastern sovereign wealth backing of the deal from Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and Abu Dhabi.
insights.speaker: ProPublica
insights.so_what: The deal's scale and foreign-sovereign financing add both antitrust and national-security review dimensions beyond the ethics issue, each an independent point of regulatory delay or blockage risk.
insights.locator: The FCC's review of the merger is one of the final hurdles

insights.claim: More than 5,000 Hollywood actors, producers, and entertainment workers (including Robert De Niro, Javier Bardem, Joaquin Phoenix, and Glenn Close) signed a letter opposing the merger on jobs and industry-independence grounds, and 12 Democratic-led states filed suit in July to block the deal under federal and state antitrust law, while the UK government has signaled its own competition investigation.
insights.speaker: ProPublica
insights.so_what: Multi-front opposition (labor, state AGs, foreign regulators) compounds the ethics-conflict risk, making a clean, fast close for the deal increasingly unlikely.
insights.locator: More than 5,000 actors, producers and entertainment workers
